"Al-Fudayl ibn Iyad, may Allah have mercy on him, said:
If you see oppressors taking revenge on other oppressors, stand and observe in astonishment.

When you see the greatest enemies of Islam in this era, those with the most blood of Muslims on their hands, turning against one another and weakening themselves through their conflicts, let your heart be soothed and turn to Allah in gratitude.

Sometimes, Allah soothes hearts by letting oppressors and tyrants orchestrate their own downfall through their conflicts and wars.

We make the wrongdoers destructive to one another because of their misdeeds. (6:129)

The parties involved in this war, the US along with their awliy, the leaders of the Gulf, as well as the Rafidah and the Yahud, are enemies, equally guilty and steeped in crimes against Islam and Muslims. Their hands are stained with the blood of countless innocents, and to true believers who grasp the depth of their kufr and the enormity of their crimes, each is as condemned as the next, none surpassing the other in guilt. They vied ruthlessly in their oppression and in betraying the Muslims.

Malik ibn Dinar, may Allah have mercy on him, said:
"I read in the revealed Books of Allah that Allah, the Exalted, said: I destroy My enemies through My enemies, and then I destroy them through My awliy."

May Allah prolong their strife so that they are preoccupied with one another and weakened, and may He raise to leadership on this earth the Muslims they betrayed, oppressed, and harmed, so that Tawheed may be established across the earth!

May Allah safeguard every innocent Muslim wherever this conflict occurs!

Ahmad Musa Jibril
2 Shawwal 1447 AH"

"Indian Muslim rulers effectively abolished Sati, not the British. It was not mere discouragement either, but an outright state enforced ban.

Europeans themselves burned some 50,000 women under the pretext of witch-hunting. Niccolao Manucci (1638-1717) described the ban as follows:

The king (Aurangzeb) issued an order that in all lands under Mogul control never again should the officials allow a woman to be burnt. This order endures to this day.

Jean de Thvenot (1633-1677) wrote how Hindu females rejoiced when this ban was imposed:

To conclude, the Women are happy that the Mahometans (Muslims) are become the Masters in the Indies, to deliver them from the tyranny of the Bramens (Brahmins).

John Ovington (1653-1731) described the ground effect of this ban as follows:

Since the Mahometans became masters of the Indies, this execrable custom is much abated, and almost laid aside, by the orders which the nabobs receive for suppressing and extinguishing it in all their provinces. And now it is very rare, except it be some Rajah's (Hindu princes) wives

This ban was so successful that the British governor general William Bentinck himself declared in the Sati Regulation Act (1829) that:

The practice of suttee... by a vast majority of that people throughout India the practice is not kept up, nor observed: in some extensive districts it does not exist

Some may be argue that Sati was not stopped completely by Muslims. To such people, it may be asked: so what? How does that transfer the credit for banning Sati to the British? Throughout the entirety of the British Raj, Sati continued to be practiced and never fully disappeared.

In fact the last Sati in India was Roop Kanwar, in 1987, or 40 years after India gained independence. In August 2009, the sixty-year old widow of Nanchhu Ram Meena of Kuchar village in Sikar district, Rajasthan, attempted to immolate herself, but was thwarted by her family, the village community, and the police.

Then why this selective credit-distributioj bias towards the Europeans? The same Europeans whose deeds were as follows:

"Ibn Battuta left home at 21 to complete a religious pilgrimage that should have taken sixteen months. He came back twenty-nine years later.

Just look at the map, he is a man that truly lived an odyssey.

I've gathered some highlights from his 117.000 kilometer journey.

Here we go:

Born February 24, 1304, in Tangier, Morocco, into a family of Islamic legal scholars and judges. His name, literally, means "son of a duckling."

Trained as a qadi, a Muslim judge with authority over religious and civil matters. That credential would open doors on every continent he visited. He was smart enough to know it.

Left home riding alone on a donkey. On the road to Mecca he developed a fever so severe he had to tie himself to his saddle to avoid collapsing. He kept going. That became the pattern.

In Alexandria, early in the journey, a holy man named Sheikh Burhanuddin told him he would travel to India, Sind, and China, and meet specific scholars there by name. Ibn Battuta did exactly that. He recorded the prophecy without apparent amazement.

Completed the hajj in 1326, then joined a caravan heading into Mesopotamia. He had discovered, somewhere along the North African coast, that he simply loved to travel.

One caravan he joined functioned as a moving city. When it stopped, food was cooked in giant brass cauldrons for the poor. It had its own markets, luxury goods, and fresh fruit. At night they lit torches along the entire length of the column, turning the darkness into what he described as radiant day.

What followed is almost impossible to compress. Persia. Iraq. Azerbaijan. Yemen. The Horn of Africa. Mogadishu. The coast of Kenya and Tanzania. The Crimea. Constantinople. Central Asia. India. The Maldives. Sri Lanka. Sumatra. China. Mali. He crossed the Sahara. He rode the Grand Canal. He visited Beijing, Hangzhou, and Guangzhou, and reportedly saw the Great Wall.

117,000 kilometers in total. That surpassed Zheng He's 50,000 and Marco Polo's 24,000. He did it without a mission, a sponsor, or a navy.

His method was elegant and entirely parasitic on a single fact: the Islamic world in 1325 stretched from Morocco to the Malay peninsula, and everywhere within it, a scholar who could speak Arabic and recite the law was guaranteed hospitality.

Ibn Battuta exploited this with genius. He arrived in courts as a learned man, was appointed qadi, collected gifts, gathered a retinue, married locally, then left.

He documented death rituals everywhere he went with the detachment of an anthropologist. In Turkey, forty days of mourning for a ruler's mother. In Iran, a funeral that resembled a wedding celebration. In some regions, slaves and concubines buried alive with the deceased. He recorded all of it and moved on.

In Delhi, the Sultan appointed him grand qadi of the city. His employer was a ruler described as an extraordinary mixture of generosity and cruelty. Ibn Battuta watched friends executed regularly, feared for his own life daily, and eventually fell into disgrace. He wrote about the sultan with a psychologist's precision, terror and fascination running through every line.

Then came the shipwreck that saved his life. Appointed as the sultan's ambassador to China, he loaded ships with hundreds of gifts including horses, slaves, and gold. He missed the departure to attend Friday prayers. The ships sank in a storm. Everything was gone. He was alive, stranded, and too afraid to return to Delhi to explain what had happened. He sailed to the Maldives instead.

In the Maldives he served as qadi, married into the ruling family, got involved in local politics, and came close to making a play for the sultanate itself. He found the situation too dangerous and moved on.

He survived bandits, shipwrecks, and a sultan's suspicion. On one occasion he was robbed and escaped with nothing but his trousers. He caught up to his caravan on foot and kept going.

By the end, the Rihla records encounters with over 60 sultans and more than 2,000 prominent figures. He made himself welcome, or at least useful, in virtually every court he entered.

He kept no journal. Everything he recorded, he carried in his head for decades. When he finally dictated the whole account, he reconstructed twenty-nine years of travel entirely from memory. 

Some scholars believe sections describing China were lifted from earlier authors. His account and Marco Polo's share suspiciously similar passages. He had no notes to prove otherwise.

Near the end of his life, the Sultan of Morocco insisted he dictate the whole account to a scholar. The result was titled A Gift to Those Who Contemplate the Wonders of Cities and the Marvels of Travelling, now known simply as the Rihla.

Then he was appointed a judge in Morocco and vanished from history. He died in 1368 or 1369. His work was unknown outside the Muslim world until the nineteenth century.

His contribution to geography is considered as great as that of any geographer, yet for centuries he appeared in no textbook, Muslim or Western.

To conclude: Ibn Battuta did not set out to be an explorer. He set out to fulfill an obligation and found he could not stop. What he produced, one man's firsthand account of the medieval Islamic world from Morocco to China, is a document with no equivalent in any other civilization of its era.

Marco Polo had backers, a trade route, and a famous name. Ibn Battuta had a credential, a memory, and an inability to go home.

As always, if you have a figure that should be honored and immortalized with a card, I'd love to hear your suggestions."

"Sayyidun Abdullh ibn Umar    was among the most devoted, scrupulous, and steadfast Companions of the Messenger of Allh . He was a man whose life became a living embodiment of the Sunnah, so precise in his adherence that he would follow even the smallest actions of the Prophet  out of love and reverence.

He was Abdullh ibn Umar ibn al-Khab al-Adaw al-Qurash, the son of the great Caliph Sayyidun Umar ibn al-Khab   . He was born in Makkah before the Hijrah and embraced Islm at a young age alongside his noble father. He emigrated to al-Madnah al-Munawwarah at the age of ten, and thus was counted among the early Muhjirn, those whom Allh, Most High, praised in His Book:

And the foremost, the first of the Muhjirn and the Anr and those who followed them with excellence, Allh is pleased with them, and they are pleased with Him, and He has prepared for them gardens beneath which rivers flow, wherein they will abide forever. That is the great success.

[Qurn 9:100]

In his youth, he longed to participate in battle alongside the Messenger of Allh . He was presented at Badr but was turned back due to his young age. He was again presented at Uud, when he was only thirteen years old, hoping to join the ranks, but he was sent back by the Prophet  due to his youth, alongside Sayyidun Usmah ibn Zayd   . That moment weighed heavily upon him, and he later said: 

A night like that never came my way, filled with sleeplessness, sadness, and weeping, because the Messenger of Allh  did not accept me.

After returning him from Uud, the Messenger of Allh  appointed him among those tasked with guarding the children in Madnah. 

Yet his heart remained attached to striving in the path of Allh, and his sincere supplication to be permitted to fight was soon answered. In the fifth year after Hijrah, when he reached the age of fifteen, he was allowed to participate in the Battle of al-Khandaq. 

From that point forward, he took part in the major campaigns of Islm. He participated in the campaign against Ban Qurayah, the journey to al-udaybiyyah, and was among those who pledged beneath the tree in the Pledge of al-Riwn, a moment honored by Allh, Most High:

Indeed, Allh was pleased with the believers when they pledged allegiance to you under the tree. He knew what was in their hearts, so He sent down tranquility upon them and rewarded them with a near victory.

[Qurn 48:18]

He later joined the campaigns of Khaybar, Mutah, the Conquest of Makkah, the expedition against Ban Judhaymah, the Battle of unayn, and the march to Tabk. He was also among the soldiers of the army of Sayyidun Usmah ibn Zayd   . 

After returning from this expedition, he volunteered in the wars against apostasy and had the honor of participating in the Battle of Yammah against Musaylamah al-Kadhdhb. 

He later marched to Shm and fought at al-Yarmk, then joined the campaigns in the Persian lands under Sayyidun Sad ibn Ab Waqq   , attending al-Qdisiyyah, Jall, and the conquest of al-Madin. He also took part in the conquest of Egypt and campaigns in North Africa. 

Even in his later years, he joined the expedition toward Constantinople, about which the Prophet  said: The first army to attack the city of Caesar is forgiven.

What distinguished Ibn Umar    above all was his extraordinary commitment to following the Sunnah. He would carefully observe where the Prophet  prayed, where he sat, and even the paths he walked, then imitate them with remarkable precision. 

If he saw the Prophet  perform prayer in a particular place, he would later pray in that very same place. If he saw him make supplication while standing, he would supplicate while standing; and if he saw him invoke Allh while sitting, he would do the same. His life was not merely one of narration, but of complete imitation.

He was among the foremost scholars of the Companions and a prolific narrator of hadith, transmitting over 2,600 narrations. His knowledge was paired with deep caution, he was known for his reluctance to issue legal verdicts without certainty, and he preferred silence over speculation. 

Due to his age, knowledge, and unwavering adherence to the Sunnah, he became known in his time as the Shaykh of the abah, and even powerful governors such as al-ajjj ibn Ysuf treated him with respect and deference due to his immense status amongst the Muslims. From the greatest testimonies to his precision in transmitting the Sunnah is that he stands at the head of one of the most authentic chains in hadith, the Golden Chain.

Mlik  Nfi  Ibn Umar.

A generation of leading Tbin took knowledge from him, including Sad ibn al-Musayyib, Thbit al-Bunn, ws, al-Zuhr, Muammad ibn Srn, Nfi (his freed servant), Mujhid, and many others. His influence thus extended deeply into the next generation of scholarship. 

His legacy was also carried forward through his own household, as several of his sons became scholars, most notably Slim ibn Abdullh ibn Umar, who was counted among the great jurists of Madnah. Through both his students and his family, his knowledge and methodology continued to shape the transmission of the Sunnah for generations.

During the periods of civil strife, having lived to witness both the First and Second Fitnah among the Muslims, Ibn Umar    consistently adopted a principled stance of restraint. He presented himself at the sittings of arbitration, demonstrating his desire for unity among the Muslims. 

He famously said: When you call me to prayer, I respond. However, when you call me to fight a Muslim, I do not. He never raised his sword against the believers. Yet, on the other hand, he answered every call to strive against the disbelievers, embodying the words of Allh, Most High:

Humble toward the believers, powerful against the disbelievers; they strive in the cause of Allh 

[Qurn 5:54]

He was known for his humility, generosity, and detachment from worldly life. Wealth would come to him, yet he would spend it freely in charity. He freed many slaves and cared deeply for the poor and needy. His nights were often spent in prayer, and his days in fasting and remembrance.

Sayyidun Abdullh ibn Umar    passed away in Makkah in 73 AH and was buried near Dh uw, leaving behind a legacy of unwavering adherence to the Sunnah and a life defined by sincerity and caution.

May Allh be pleased with Sayyidun Abdullh ibn Umar   , the devoted follower of the Sunnah and one of the greatest scholars among the Companions of the Messenger of Allh ."

""Pakistan hosted the U.S.-Iran negotiations in Islamabad for one overriding reason: survival.

A day before the talks began, Saudi Arabia and Qatar announced $5 billion in financial assistance to Pakistan. The same day, Saudi Arabias defence ministry confirmed that Pakistani fighter jets had landed at King Abdulaziz Air Base under the mutual defence pact signed in September 2025.

These two important details reveal the transactional nature of the Pakistan-Saudi-Qatar relations that put Pakistan in an precarious position. 

Pakistan shares a 900km border with Iran. It has watched Iranian ballistic missiles and drones strike Saudi and GCC targets for weeks. Its economy is in intensive care, dependent on IMF lifelines and Gulf deposits to keep foreign reserves above collapse thresholds. It cannot afford a war with Iran. Militarily, Pakistan has the hardware. Financially, it would be ruined.

The 2025 defence pact with Saudi Arabia states that aggression against one is aggression against both. That pact now obliges Pakistan to act. Saudi Arabia has been struck almost daily by Iran for more than a month now. 

Mediation was Pakistans escape route. A successful deal in Islamabad would have ended the war and neutralised the pacts trigger. No war means no obligation to get involved and no Iranian missiles aimed at Pakistani soil. Pakistan gets to keep the $5 billion, stay out of the conflict, and maintain relations with Tehran.

But the talks have failed. Vance left Islamabad without a deal after 21 hours of negotiations. The ceasefire is fragile and Strait of Hormuz remains contested.

Now Pakistan faces the worst of all outcomes. It has taken the money. Its jets are on Saudi soil. The defence pact is active. And the war is not over.

$5 billion does not cover the cost of a war with Iran. It barely covers Pakistans debt repayment to the UAE this month. It is a down payment on compliance, not compensation for the devastation that would follow.

Pakistan must now make a choice: honour the pact and enter a war it cannot sustain, or refuse and break with Riyadh. There is no middle ground left."

"Regarding Pakistan / Afghanistan relations 

From my friend @LongshotIbrahim -- a brother who has written on and studied the subject considerably:

I have mentioned this before but in 1980s/1990s various Afghan factions, incl both Taliban and some of their foes, supported Pakistan and Kashmir and went out of the way to do so. This was while an increasingly pro-India US was running interference for India.

While Afg mujahideen in general backed Pakistan and Kashmir, outstanding case was Hikmatyar (Afg prime minister in 1993-94, then 1995-96), and his field commander Khalid Faruqi. Hizbul Mujahideen were trained and at least partly modelled on them for a decade or so.

Another major case was Arsala-Rahmani Daulat (prime minister 1994-95), who later joined Taliban and was murdered in 2012. A third was justice minister Jalaluddin Haqqani, father of today's Taliban interior minister Sirajuddin.

In general Taliban remained pro-Pakistan thru their first emirate. During the 1999 Pak war w India over Kashmir, Taliban prime minister Mohammad Rabbani offered to send thousands of fighters (but was declined bcos there wasnt really a manpower shortage).

At the end of 1999, Pak militants related to Kashmir jihad incl. Masood Azhar were released from Indian custody in a hostage exchange mediated, much to Pakistani glee, by Taliban Kandahar corps commander Akhtar Usmani.

Many of Kashmir-focused mujahideen also supported Taliban in late 1990s incl Saifullah Akhtar and AbdulJabbar Farooq. They fought directly under defence minister Ubaidullah Akhund and his lieutenant, today's Afghan security minister Ibrahim Sadar.

In east Afg, Taliban and Hizb and also Salafi networks were linked to Kashmir-focused mujahideen incl Salafi "Lashkar Taiba". One commander in Kunar who latee fought US at the famed Korengal valley, AbdulRahim, was a Hizbi turned Taliban but of Salafi persuasion, close to Taiba.

In 2001-02 after US invasion, Pakistan banned some of these groups (but tacitly tolerated others like Taiba). AbdulJabbar and Saifullah as well as Ilyas Kashmiri revolted against Pakistan, while Khalil and Azhar nervously walked a fine line.

In 2004, partly bcos of these rebels but also largely to distract US after Iran/Libya tattled, Pakistani army invaded Waziristan region. The army was surprised at local tribal backlash and adopted the line "Why are yoy fighting us, go and fight US in Afghanistan"

This line was supported by Afghan Taliban and practically every one of their leaders - emir Umar, Jalaluddin and Sirajuddin, Usmani, Daddullah Lang, Ubaidullah, chief justice NurMohammad Saqib, Mohammad Yasir, Sadar Azam - mediated on Pakistan's behalf with Waziristan insurgency.

However US intelligence (CIA) infiltrated Pakistan in 2005, using earthquake relief and humanitarian aid as a cover. Their targets were not only Taliban or Qaeda but also Pakistani groups linked to Kashmir, such as Taiba

Since the late 1990s US stance on Kashmir had been "Pakistan should forget Kashmir, make peace with India, and focus on its own Islamic radicalism", and this is what US (and their local lapdogs in Pakistan) pursued.

US also repeatedly sabotaged deals and ceasefires that, mediated by Taliban, army had made with insurgency in Waziristan. Ie it was US, not Taliban, who put Pakistan army and civilians at risk; it was US, not Taliban, who undermined Kashmir issue

Despite this Pakistan scrambled to appease and placate US, who would constantly bark "Do more" and undermine Pakistan further. It wasnt until 2011 that CIA agents were expelled as US/Pak relations hit a low and by then US was cracking down on Kashmir activists too

Meanwhile despite the Pak/US relations various Taliban networks incl Sirajuddin Haqqani continued to hit India; e.g. in 2008 Indian attache Ravi Mehta was killed at Kabul in a joint op w/ Taiba.

Sirajuddin was particularly hated by US for his role in a "triple agent" trick that killed CIA field commander Jennifer Matthews in what is still regarded as one of the most humiliating episodes in CIA history.

One CIA agent from this period - when agency was systematically destabilizing Pakistan, vilifying Kashmir, and bolstering India - was Sarah Adams, who has a particular visceral hatred for Sirajuddin Haqqani even by the agency's standards

Now bear in mind Adams' background. She was working for CIA in region at a time when it was actively involved in what even the most charitable appraisal could call anti-Pakistan/anti-Kashmir activity, during a period when Taliban were doing what Pakistan requested incl. v India.

however, after 2022 Pak coup - which was at least partly done for US approval - Pakistani pseudo-nationalists have embraced Adams as an unimpeachable source whenever she blames Taliban for anything, bcos they are eager to offload any responsibility onto "those ingrate Afghans"

Adams blames Taliban for everything, and she does this in large part bcos of her background and her resentment at Haqqanis, in particular. In 2023 she claimed that Sirajuddin had masterminded Hamas/Hezbollah war v Israel (oh, of course she also hates Palestine & loves Israel)

Adams doesn't give a shit abt Kashmir. She is pro-Israel, and Israel is pro-India, and India is anti-Kashmir. Her CIA career was spent partly in pro-India/anti-Kashmir activity. However, she knows that Pakistanis care about Kashmir and she wants to turn Pakistanis against Taliban

so after pro-US 2022 coup in Pakistan, Adams gets onto podcasts and "exposes" her target: "Oh, Taliban betrayed Kashmiris; oh, Taliban backstabbed Pakistan". If she could, she'd accuse Taliban of murdering Liaquat Ali-Khan in 1951 in order to whip up anti-Afghan sentiment in Pak

The fact is Taliban have a long record of supporting Pakistan and even Kashmir, and a looong record of opposing India at least on this issue. Whereas US,and its agents like Adams, have exactly the opposite record. But to justify current Pak war v Afg, she spreads these daft tales

"Should We Forgive Everyone Who Harmed Us?

"During the past Ramadan, amidst many events and responsibilities, I found myself one day hesitant to visit my mother, may Allah forgive her and grant her Firdaws. Fatigue had set in, and the schedule was overwhelming. Yet I felt unusually compelled to go, it was Friday, after Asr, and something in my heart told me I must.

Upon arriving, I noticed two young brothers waiting patiently nearby. They approached me, and one of them told me he had learned that this visit was part of my daily routine. He had researched the cemetery, located my mothers grave in that vast space, found the exact plot, and had been waiting there for most of the day, after traveling by plane solely to meet me.
May Allah forgive my mother, grant her al-Firdaws, and reward these brothers.

He told me he had made du to meet me, based on a certain stipulation about me: if our meeting was the answer to his du due to that specific condition, it would be all I could wish for dunya and akhira.

At the time, I was hesitant, this is, after all, a confirmed tactic the FBI has used dozens of times to entrap me. And I was also too occupied to sit with him properly, so he extended his stay. When I finally sat with him, saw his radiant face (Allhumma brik lahu), and heard his questions, I regretted not having dropped everything earlier to spend all my time with him.

His manners were remarkable, truly beyond his years. He was very young, only 18. His questions were the kind you love to hear: thoughtful, sincere, and filled with a genuine desire for knowledge. He wasnt manhaj-shopping or hopping, nor was he chasing controversies or indulging in te back-and-forth games. He came to learn the dn of Allah with humility and seriousness, and Allah knows best what is in the hearts.

As I dropped him off at the airport, I advised him to begin following the lessons, since he mentioned he hadnt kept up with all the classes. He took that advice sincerely, he began studying and taking notes. Today, a video was sent to me of him completing the Tawheed class.

As he was leaving, he handed me a beautifully bound notebook filled with poems he had written, for me and about me. Allah is my witness: I am not worthy of a single letter within its pages. Yet it spoke volumes about the kind of heart he possesses.
O Allah, make me better than they think of me, forgive me for what they do not know, and do not hold me accountable for what they say.

When he would send me questions, months might pass before I could respond, yet he would wait patiently. May Allah bless him and forgive me for my shortcomings toward him.

When a debate was later announced, he didnt play foolish games seeking attention, nor did he cozy up with the enemies, serving their evil agenda while pretending to be on the side of the truth. Instead, he knew his duty, simple and clear: to stand behind the truth.

Out of the hundreds of emails, texts, and calls I receive daily, he was, in fact, the only one who privately sent me a du by text regarding this matter, one that I woke up to. It was so beautifully worded, so sincere and heartfelt, that it genuinely soothed my heart.

It reminded me of the story of Qutaybah ibn Muslim and the du of Muammad ibn Wsi.
Ibn al-Jawz mentioned in ifat al-afwah that Muammad ibn Wsi was once with Qutaybah ibn Muslim in an army. Qutaybah was the governor of Khursn at the time, and the Turks had come out to confront them. So Qutaybah sent someone to the Masjid to see who was there. He was told, No one is there except Muammad ibn Wsi, raising his finger in duaa'.
Qutaybah then said:
That finger of his is more beloved to me than thirty thousand cavalry.
And in Siyar Alm al-Nubal, Imm al-Dhahab reported that Qutaybah said:
That finger is more beloved to me than one hundred thousand drawn swords and strong young fighters.

I ask Allah to reward his parents for such a noble upbringing, to keep him and his family steadfast, and to make him an lim and an imm of the Muslimeen.
May Allah honor me and surround me with such brothers. It is men of such character who, by the will of Allah, will be the catalysts of the awaited victory.
 
Once he completes what I advised him to pursue, it is brothers like this, sincere, disciplined, and grounded, whom I would be honored to teach. In fact, I would be the one asking to teach them.

On a side note: Many brothers have traveled from across the U.S., and even from abroadto visit. May Allah reward the sincere among them. However, please only do so with my personal approval. I am not like others who have the support of Masaajid, means, resources, or the ease to accommodate so many visitors or students.

Ahmad Musa Jibril
Rabi II 17, 1447 AH"

"One of the most shameless lies still told about colonialism is that European powers gifted Africa its roads, its schools, its hospitals. Shut up! You gifted us nothing. We built it, we paid for it, we bled for it.
Those roads were not built so African farmers could trade with each other or so African communities could grow. They were built to move our minerals and our crops from the interior to the ports and ship them to Europe.

 Every kilometre of colonial railway followed the same logic: not to serve us, but to drain us. The hospitals were built to keep labourers alive enough to keep working, not because colonial administrators believed African lives had value, but because a sick worker interrupts the extraction schedule. My grandmother was denied treatment for her twins dying of smallpox because my grandfather was in prison for resisting colonial rule. She lost one of them.

And who built any of it? Our grandparents. Forced, beaten, worked into the ground under quotas, mutilated when they failed to meet them. When someone calls that a gift, what they are really asking is that we thank our oppressors for the infrastructure our own suffering produced.

We also paid for it in cash. In 1932, French colonial commissioner Robert de Guise imposed new taxes on Togolese people whose incomes had already collapsed by nearly sixty percent during the Great Depression. When women dared to protest, France shipped 174 colonial soldiers from Cte d'Ivoire to crush them. Girls as young as thirteen were raped and 12 protesters were killed. That is how the roads, the schools, the administrative buildings, the hospitals were financed: with our blood. Not European generosity.

And when independence finally came, the colonisers left with a bill. They calculated the cost of everything they had built through our coerced labour and our taxed income, called it colonial debt, and demanded repayment from the very nations they had spent a century looting. We paid for our own exploitation. Twice!

In Europe, when a government builds a road, no citizen is asked to be grateful. It is called public service. But when colonisers built infrastructure on our land, with our bodies, with our money, after killing and raping us, we are expected to call it the "benefits of colonialism". The audacity!"

Max Holway, aged 54, from Boyne Park in Tunbridge Wells, previously worked at Tonbridge Police Station but left the force in March of the previous year. His departure followed concerns related to inappropriate use of the Kent Police computer system to access pornography.

Holway was arrested on July 27 after employees at a computer repair shop in Tunbridge Wells discovered illegal material on a device he had brought in. The staff reported their findings to the authorities, leading to an investigation.

It was later revealed that Holway had purchased the computer online and downloaded the images over a period of approximately six hours. The offences included possession of 24 category B images, which are considered among the more serious, as well as 21,824 category C images and 30 video clips also classified as category C.

During the investigation, Holway initially attempted to attribute responsibility to his lodger but later admitted to the offences.

At Maidstone Crown Court, Judge Charles Macdonald QC acknowledged Holways previous good character but emphasized the seriousness of the crimes. The judge stated that a custodial sentence was necessary to serve as a deterrent.

In addition to his prison sentence, Holway will be placed on the sex offenders register for ten years. He is also prohibited from working with children or vulnerable adults in the future."

"Salafis do not believe that the pagans of Makkah possessed a complete understanding of Tawheed al-Rububiyyah. When they assert that the pagans affirmed Tawheed al-Rububiyyah, they are referring to its foundational aspect (asl), which is the belief in one God as the ultimate creator and sustainer of the universe, the only being who is entirely independent. Salafis emphasize this point in response to saint venerators who argue that they are not guilty of shirk simply because they do not consider their saints to be independent of Allah. Salafis contend that the independence of a being is not the sole criterion for determining whether certain beliefs or actions constitute major shirk.

Salafis are well aware that the pagan Quraysh, while acknowledging Allah as the supreme creator, also attributed daughters to Him, worshipped idols, and failed to affirm His attributes correctly. These theological errors are considered distortions of Tawheed al-Rububiyyah.

Prominent Salafi scholars, such as Shaykh Abdulrahman Al-Barrak and Shaykh Yusuf Al-Ghafees, have argued that saint venerators also commit shirk in Rububiyyah by ascribing certain powers to their saints, powers that should be attributed exclusively to Allah.

To avoid semantic confusion, if Rububiyyah is primarily understood as Allahs independence and self-sufficiency, then Salafis assert that most of the pagan Quraysh acknowledged this foundational aspect. (However, Salafi scholars such as Shaykh Sultan al-Umayri, Abdulrazzaq Abu Badr, and Abu Bakr Muhammad Zakariyyah note that the Quraysh were not a homogeneous group and differed in their beliefs.) If Rububiyyah is defined in broader terms, while still rooted in the foundation of Allahs independence, Salafis maintain that the pagans only affirmed this foundational aspect.

The central issue, according to Salafis, is the affirmation of Allahs independence and the rejection of any justification by saint venerators that their actions do not constitute major shirk."

"There are men whose voices only rise when the winds of public opinion begin to blow, and there are men whose words precede the storm itself. Men who speak when it is difficult, when it is unpopular, when it carries a price. From the signs of a true Alim is not that he reacts to events, but that he forewarns the Ummah before the events even unfold. You can mention a calamity, a trial, a tribulation that has struck this Ummah over the past four decades  except that a record exists, a reminder preserved, where Shaykh Ahmad had already spoken, already warned, already clarified. This is not coincidence by the way this is Basirh, insight granted by Allh to those who cling firmly to revelation.

Allh says:

         
O you who believe, if you fear Allh, He will grant you a criterion. (Surah al-Anfl 8:29)

This furqn is not merely knowledge it is the ability to distinguish truth from falsehood when the masses are confused, when emotions are high, when deception is widespread. And this is what we witnessed. 

When the issue of Falastn suddenly became a global trend, when voices rose and slogans filled the air, Shaykh Ahmad did not need to scramble to speak because he had already addressed it years prior, almost a decade earlier, clarifying its reality, its roots, and the correct Islamic stance upon it. While others rode the wave, he had already charted the course.

The Messenger of Allh  said:

             
Indeed, Allh does not take away knowledge by snatching it from the people, but He takes away knowledge by taking the scholars. 

So look carefully, when a scholar speaks consistently, courageously, and with foresight, this is a nimah that must be recognized before it is lost.

When people rushed toward the ballot boxes, placing hope in systems not built upon the Shariah, he had already spoken  not once, but repeatedly  exposing the foundational deviation in such systems, warning against the dilution of Tawhid and the subtle normalization of shirk in governance. While others justified, softened, or remained silent, he stood firm upon principle.

And when the tides of confusion intensified  in the recent affairs involving the Rfidah and the situation tied to Iran  again, this was not something new to him. Long before many understood in the West, he had already exposed their history of oppression, their crimes in Shm, their treachery against Ahl al-Sunnah. He spoke when it was not trending, when it did not bring followers, when it did not align with popular narratives.

When the blood of the Muslims in Ghouta was spilled, when oppression crushed the weak, his voice was present. When the tyranny of the Taghut of Egypt, Sisi, unfolded, he did not veil the truth. 

This is the reality of the scholar who fears Allh not the reactions of people, nor the consequences of speaking truth.

And here lies the difference between those who inherit the legacy of the Prophets, and those who bend under pressure, who remain silent to preserve status, or who speak only when it is safe. The Murjiah and their ilk wish that their scholars possessed even a fraction of this courage but truth is not granted to every tongue, nor is firmness gifted to every heart."

"The most exalted day of the year for du has dawned  the blessed Day of Arafah.
Prepare your heart and your du from now, for this is a day when du are answered, hopes are fulfilled, sins are forgiven, faults are pardoned, and souls are freed from the Fire.

Approach this day with profound humility. Renounce reliance on your own might, and acknowledge that all strength and power belong solely to Allah. Embrace the truth of human frailty and surrender entirely to Him.

Let your supplications flow abundantly and unceasingly. Immerse your heart and tongue in dhikr, du, and heartfelt recitation of the Quran. It is reported that among the pious predecessors, some would spend the Day of Arafah seated in deep remembrance and prayer, yearning for the mercy of their Lord.

Make your du lengthy and persistent. Return again and again with your most intimate personal requests  and do not forget the oppressed and the afflicted among this Ummah.

Know with Yaqeen that when Allah grants, His generosity surpasses all imagination. The arrows of du on the Day of Arafah  by Allahs infinite permission  never miss their mark. 

Whoever remains steadfast in du shall be granted enduring joy and fiyah. 

The great scholar Ibn Abd al-Barr said:

The supplication on the Day of Arafah is better than on any other day and the du of Arafah is, in most cases, accepted.

And inLatif al-Marif, Ibn Rajab relates that one of the righteous said:

By Allah, I have never made a du on the Day of Arafah, and a year passed without seeing it answered  as clearly as the break of dawn.

The Day of Arafah is a majestic and awe-inspiring day  a day when the signs of Allahs mercy are clearly manifest.
It presents a sacred opportunity to renew ones faith, realign ones path, and make a sincere, heartfelt resolution to repent and submit wholly to Allah  not only for the coming year, but for a lifetime.

As virtuous as Laylat al-Qadr is, we do not know which night among the last ten it truly is.But we do know the Day of Arafah  the 9th of Dhul-Hijjah  and this year, it falls onThursday, tomorrow.

On Laylat al-Qadr, Jibrl and the angels descend, and that is a tremendous virtue.But on the Day of Arafah,Allah descendsin a manner befitting His Majesty.

It is a day when the signs ofTawdare renewed and exalted, and when pure servitude to Allah alone  without any partner  is fully realized.

That is why it is mentioned that among the recommended supplications for the Day of Arafah is a du ofTawd:

L ilha illa Allh, wadahu l sharka lah, lahu al-mulku wa lahu al-amd, wa huwa al kulli shayin qadr.There is no god but Allah, alone without partner. His is the dominion and His is the praise, and He has power over all things.

One must be vigilant not to fall short on this sacred day  for it is a moment that cannot be reclaimed, an opportunity that may never come again.

Dont forget to fast on this blessed day (if you are not performing Hajj), for it expiates the sins of the previous year and the year to come.

Ya Allah, let not the Day of Arafah pass except that You have lifted from us every burden, eased our affairs, and answered our du.

Ya Allah, Most Compassionate, swiftly grant freedom to the imprisoned Muslims wherever they may be, and free our sisters enduring hardship and suffering in the camps.
Ya Allah, Protector of the helpless, shield our children, our brothers, and our sisters in Gaza and elsewhere from harm and despair.
Ya Allah, bring down theawghtwho spread corruption and injustice far and wide  those who conspired with the disbelievers against the believers in Gaza and in Baghuz.
Ya Allah, grant the righteous and sincereMuwaidna clear, resounding victory.
Ya Allah, Most Merciful, envelop us all in Your boundless pardon, infinite gentleness, and overwhelming mercy.

Ahmad Musa Jibril
Dhul-Hijjah 8, 1446"

"The war on Gaza has not ceased  it has merely morphed into grimmer, more insidious forms.

The bombs still rain from the sky. The children still perish. The siege tightens with each passing day.

A Northamptonshire Police sergeant has received a 12month suspended prison sentence after admitting to making 80 indecent images of children, including material involving girls as young as four.

Ross Coleman, 42, was sentenced at Leicester Crown Court after pleading guilty to three offences of making indecent images between October 2016 and October 2018. Judge Rhona Campbell told him he had provided a market for images of the sexual abuse of children, stressing that such crimes directly contribute to the exploitation of vulnerable victims.

Police executed a warrant at Colemans former family home in Higham Ferrers. Officers seized several items, including his personal mobile phone, which contained thumbnail images linked to videos he had streamed to his Smart TV.

Over a twoyear period, Coleman downloaded 80 files depicting child sexual abuse. Fortyfive were classified as category A, the most serious level. A further 20 were category B and 15 category C. Investigators also found more than 100 additional files with similar names that had been accessed during the same timeframe.

Coleman was sentenced to 12 months imprisonment, suspended for 18 months. He must complete 120 hours of unpaid work and 35 days of rehabilitation activity. He is also subject to a Sexual Harm Prevention Order restricting his access to the internet.

Judge Campbell emphasised the realworld harm caused by such offending, telling the court that every image represented a child who had been abused and would carry the impact for life.

Coleman resigned from Northamptonshire Police in February. Following the conclusion of the criminal case, he will now face a gross misconduct hearing.

Detective Constable Jason Cullum, who led the investigation, said the outcome should serve as a warning to anyone who believes they can view indecent images without consequence. He noted that offenders risk losing their families, homes and employment, as Coleman did."

"When ilm is divorced from real struggle, and ibdah is severed from responsibility, devotion becomes performance and piety turns hollow. History shows that when knowledge is reduced to commentary, and worship is reduced to spectacle, the Ummah weakens, no matter how long the prayers or how loud the slogans.
Throughout Islamic history, the greatest scholars never flattered excess, theatrics, or escapism cloaked in religiosity. They exposed it, especially when it distracted the Ummah from its frontiers and obligations.

The Amr al-Muminn in adth, the scholar who encompassed every virtue except the companionship of the Prophet , Abdullh ibn alMubrak (r.a.) was once informed of certain ascetics who had taken up residence in Baghdad, becoming known for excess in worship and outward displays of devotion. He condemned them, as narrated by Jubaysh ibn Ab alWard, saying:

Oh you ascetics! Those who have donned wool and sacrificed animals merely to be counted among the worshippers.
Hold firmly to the frontiers and worship therein.
Baghdad is not the place for ascetics.
Baghdad is a resting-place for kings,
and a gathering-place and resort for hunters. (Trikh Baghdd) 

This was not a dismissal of worship, but a denunciation of misplaced devotion. Not a rebuke of asceticism, but of performative piety divorced from duty. Not an attack on spirituality, but on withdrawal while the Ummah bleeds.

Ibn al-Mubrak (r.a.) understood a truth many prefer to forget: worship that ignores obligation is not elevation, it is evasion. When oppression rages, frontiers collapse, and the Ummah is violated, retreating into comfortable piety is not neutrality, it is abandonment. True zuhd is not found in comfort, applause, or city circles. It is forged where responsibility is heaviest; where risk is real; where faith demands sacrifice, not spectatorship. It is worship that carries weight, consequence, and cost.

What would the great Abdullh ibn al-Mubrak (r.a.) say about us today?

He would not be impressed by packed Masjid that fall silent on injustice. He would not be moved by tears shed in du while hands remain idle from action. He would not praise those who debate theology endlessly yet recoil from responsibility. He would say that we have perfected the rituals and abandoned the burden. That we have learned to speak Islam fluently while fearing it's implications."
